title: Modelling — graphical, physical and CAD
summary: This lesson explores the fundamental role of modelling in the design process. It covers the three main types: graphical, physical, and Computer-Aided Design (CAD), evaluating their respective advantages, disadvantages, and applications within the IB Design Technology curriculum.
learningObjectives: Identify and describe graphical, physical, and CAD modelling techniques.
Compare and contrast the use of different modelling methods in various design contexts.
Analyse the role of modelling in communicating ideas and gathering feedback from clients and users.
Evaluate the fidelity, cost, and time implications of selecting a particular modelling technique.

simpleExplanation.title: From Idea to Object: The Designer's Toolkit
simpleExplanation.summary: Modelling is how designers turn abstract ideas into something tangible or visual that can be tested and shared. It's a crucial process of refinement, moving from rough concepts to detailed, functional products.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine you're planning to build an amazing treehouse. You wouldn't just start nailing planks to a tree. First, you'd sketch your idea on paper (graphical model) to see how it looks. Then, you might build a small version out of cardboard and sticks (physical model) to check its stability and layout. Finally, you might use a computer program (CAD model) to create precise blueprints before buying the wood. Each model helps you solve problems and improve the design before you commit to the final build.
steps: Begin with rapid ideation using 2D and 3D graphical sketches to explore different forms and concepts.
Create low-fidelity physical models from inexpensive materials like card or foam to test ergonomics and scale.
Develop a detailed digital version using Computer-Aided Design (CAD) software for precision and easy modification.
Use the CAD model to generate high-fidelity prototypes via 3D printing or to create manufacturing instructions.

flashcards.back: A 2D or 3D visualisation of an idea on a surface, such as a sketch or a technical drawing. It's used to communicate design concepts and details.
A three-dimensional, tangible representation of a design or part of a design. It can be a scale model, aesthetic model, or functional prototype.
A digital 3D model created using Computer-Aided Design software. It allows for high precision, simulation, and direct integration with manufacturing processes (CAM).
A type of graphical model that shows a 2D view of an object from one or more planes (e.g., front, top, side views). It is used for conveying precise dimensions for manufacturing.
A 3D graphical representation of an object where all three axes appear at 120 degrees to each other. It provides a pictorial view without perspective distortion.
A physical model that is a smaller or larger representation of the final product, maintaining accurate proportions. Used to visualise overall shape and context.
A physical model that looks and feels like the final product but does not function. It is used to evaluate visual appeal, form, and texture with clients and users.
A physical model built to test the performance and functionality of a design. It may not look like the final product but demonstrates how it works.

The degree to which a model represents the final product. Low-fidelity models (e.g., sketches, card models) are quick and cheap, while high-fidelity models (e.g., CAD, functional prototypes) are detailed and realistic.

A type of CAD modelling where objects are created as solid volumes. This allows for the calculation of mass, volume, and centre of gravity, and is essential for engineering analysis and manufacturing.
A type of CAD modelling that defines only the exterior surfaces of an object, without internal volume. It is often used for creating complex, free-form, and aesthetically focused shapes.
A graphical model that shows the components of an assembly separated but in their correct positions and orientations relative to each other. It's used to show how parts fit together.

faq.q: What is the main difference between an aesthetic model and a functional prototype?
faq.a: The main difference is their purpose. An aesthetic model is designed to show what the product will look and feel like (its form, colour, texture) but it doesn't work. A functional prototype is designed to test if the product works, but it may not look like the final version.

faq.q: Is a 3D print a physical model or a CAD model?
faq.a: This is a great question that highlights the link between them. The digital file is the CAD model. The 3D print itself is a physical model that has been created from the data in the CAD model. So, you use a CAD model to create a 3D-printed physical model.

faq.q: Why shouldn't a designer just start with CAD modelling immediately?
faq.a: While CAD is powerful, it can be restrictive during initial ideation. Sketching (graphical modelling) is much faster for exploring a wide variety of rough ideas. Jumping straight to the precision of CAD can make a designer focus too much on details too early, potentially limiting creativity and the exploration of more radical concepts.

sections.content: Modelling is the language of designers. It is the bridge between a thought in someone's mind and a product in the real world. In Design Technology, we categorise models into three main families: graphical, physical, and computer-aided. Understanding when and why to use each type is a critical skill for any designer, enabling effective communication, testing, and refinement throughout the design cycle.

Graphical models are visual representations of ideas, typically created on paper or a screen. They range from quick, conceptual sketches used for brainstorming (ideation) to highly detailed, formal technical drawings like orthographic projections used for manufacturing. Their primary purpose is to communicate visual information quickly and effectively.

items: **Types:** Freehand sketches, perspective drawings, isometric projections, orthographic drawings, exploded views.
**Advantages:** Quick to produce, low cost, excellent for communicating initial ideas, can be easily shared and annotated.
**Disadvantages:** Can be ambiguous or misinterpreted, lack of tangibility (cannot be held or physically tested), may not accurately represent scale or material properties.
**Application:** Primarily used in the early stages of the design process for exploring concepts and in the later stages for creating manufacturing blueprints.

sections.content: Physical models are tangible, three-dimensional objects that represent a design. Their fidelity can vary enormously. A low-fidelity model might be made from cardboard and tape to test the basic form and size of a product (e.g., a new phone). A high-fidelity functional prototype, on the other hand, might be an almost-working version of that phone, used for user testing and technical evaluation. These models allow designers and users to interact with the idea in a physical space.

items: **Types:** Scale models (for context), aesthetic models (for look and feel), functional prototypes (for testing mechanisms).

**Advantages:** Provides a tangible understanding of the design, allows for ergonomic and usability testing, facilitates feedback from non-technical stakeholders, can be used to test materials and mechanisms.
**Disadvantages:** Can be time-consuming and expensive to create (especially high-fidelity), modifications can be difficult, may not be durable.
**Application:** Used throughout the design process, from testing initial concepts with block models to final user trials with functional prototypes.

sections.question: A design team is developing a new, portable coffee maker for campers. Justify the use of both a graphical model and a physical model during the early stages of development. [4 marks]
sections.solution:
Here is a possible mark-scheme breakdown:

**Graphical Model (2 marks):**
*   **[1 mark] Identification & Description:** The team could use a series of annotated sketches (a graphical model) to quickly explore a wide range of different forms and mechanisms for the coffee maker.
*   **[1 mark] Justification:** This is advantageous because it is a fast and low-cost method to generate and communicate many ideas without committing resources. It allows the team to visualise concepts like how it packs away or how the user interacts with it, before building anything.

**Physical Model (2 marks):**
*   **[1 mark] Identification & Description:** The team could create a low-fidelity physical model using foam or cardboard to represent the most promising design from the sketches.
*   **[1 mark] Justification:** This is crucial for a portable product as it allows the team and potential users to test the ergonomics, such as the grip, weight, and packed size. This tactile feedback cannot be obtained from a sketch alone and helps validate the 'portability' requirement.

sections.content: CAD involves using specialised software to create highly detailed and precise 2D or 3D models. There are two main types of 3D CAD: solid modelling, which defines objects as solid volumes, and surface modelling, which defines the outer 'skin'. CAD models are not just visual; they are data-rich files that can be used for complex simulations (e.g., stress analysis), creating photorealistic renders, and generating instructions for manufacturing processes like 3D printing (CAM).

items: **Types:** 2D CAD (e.g., AutoCAD), 3D Solid Modelling (e.g., Fusion 360, SolidWorks), 3D Surface Modelling (e.g., Rhino).

**Advantages:** Extremely high precision and accuracy, easy to modify and iterate, can be used for virtual testing and simulation (FEA), facilitates collaboration, direct link to CAM for rapid prototyping and production.
**Disadvantages:** High initial cost for software and powerful hardware, requires significant training and expertise, can stifle creativity in the very early, fluid stages of ideation.
**Application:** Widely used in the development and manufacturing stages for detailed design, analysis, and production planning.

sections.content: In Paper 2, when asked to compare or evaluate modelling techniques, always use the context of the question. Don't just list generic pros and cons. For example, for a 'one-off, bespoke piece of jewellery', a physical wax model might be better than CAD for capturing the artistic feel. For a 'mass-produced engine part', CAD is essential for its precision and link to manufacturing. Your justification is key.

sections.question: A company is finalising the design for a new skateboard truck. Compare and contrast the use of a solid CAD model with a high-fidelity functional prototype for final performance testing. [6 marks]
sections.solution:
Here is a possible mark-scheme breakdown:

**Solid CAD Model:**
*   **[1 mark] Advantage:** The CAD model can be used for Finite Element Analysis (FEA) to simulate stresses on the truck under extreme loads (e.g., landing a jump). This can identify potential weak points virtually, without destroying a physical part.
*   **[1 mark] Disadvantage:** A simulation is only as good as its input data. It cannot perfectly replicate the complex, real-world dynamic forces of a skater's movement or unpredictable impacts, which might lead to overlooked failure modes.

**High-Fidelity Functional Prototype:**
*   **[1 mark] Advantage:** A physical prototype (e.g., CNC-machined from aluminium) allows for real-world testing by an actual skateboarder. This provides invaluable qualitative feedback on the 'feel', turning response, and durability that cannot be simulated.
*   **[1 mark] Disadvantage:** Creating this prototype is expensive and time-consuming. If it fails during testing, a new one must be manufactured, causing delays. Destructive testing also means the prototype is consumed.

**Comparison/Contrast (2 marks):**
*   **[1 mark] Cost & Time:** The CAD simulation is faster and cheaper for iterative testing of different geometries, whereas the physical prototype is costly and slow to create but provides more authentic results.
*   **[1 mark] Data Type:** The CAD model provides quantitative data (e.g., stress in megapascals), while the prototype provides both quantitative (e.g., wear measurement) and crucial qualitative data (e.g., user feedback on performance). A comprehensive testing strategy would use both.
